title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a chef called Omar talking about a community cooking programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a chef called Omar talking about a community cooking programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A chef called Omar talks about a community cooking programme.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. I’m Omar, and I organise Cooking Together, a project which teaches practical kitchen skills while preparing meals for people in our neighbourhood. We began in a restaurant kitchen which was available on Monday mornings, but it was not easy for the public to reach. We now meet in the kitchen at the [9]sports centre[/9], where there is step-free access and a bus stop outside. The project is open to adults, but our largest group of volunteers is currently made up of [10]teenagers[/10], many of whom want experience before taking hospitality courses.||New volunteers sometimes worry that they must already know lots of dishes. In fact, we start with basic skills, such as cutting vegetables carefully and measuring ingredients. Nobody needs to remember instructions: each learner is given a set of [11]recipe cards[/11] to take home and use again. Before touching any ingredients, all participants complete a brief form. We do not ask about favourite meals, but it is essential to tell us about [12]food allergies[/12], because we need to keep everyone safe. A trained leader supervises each group, and knives are only used after a demonstration.||Most of the food we prepare is for local people who cannot easily cook for themselves. In the first few sessions, we made pasta dishes, but these are not always easy to deliver without spilling. The meal requested most frequently by the organisation we support is [13]vegetable soup[/13], as it can be frozen and reheated easily. All ingredients are provided, and volunteers do not need to bring pans or other equipment. For hygiene reasons, everyone is issued with clean [14]aprons[/14] at the beginning of a session; you may bring your own, but it must be washed before use.||Once the food is prepared, it must reach the right people. We originally expected volunteers to take food on foot to nearby houses, but some journeys are too far and the meals are heavy. Instead, the project works with a local [15]delivery service[/15] that transports labelled containers each afternoon. Meals that are not needed immediately are put in a large [16]freezer[/16] in our storeroom, where the date and ingredients are recorded. Learners are welcome to stay after cooking and help with labelling, though this is not compulsory. We also run talks: the one most requested by students this year is about preparing [17]budget meals[/17], because many will soon be living away from home.||Cooking Together is not all work. At the end of June, volunteers and the families we support will meet in the park for a [18]picnic[/18]. Last year we had a formal evening meal, but people found the outdoor event more relaxed and easier for children to attend. There will be music, games and, naturally, food made by our teams. Anyone who joins before May can help plan it. I hope this has given you a clear idea of the programme, and I will now take any questions about joining.

dialogue.speaker: omar
dialogue.text: Good morning. I’m Omar, and I organise Cooking Together, a project which teaches practical kitchen skills while preparing meals for people in our neighbourhood. We began in a restaurant kitchen which was available on Monday mornings, but it was not easy for the public to reach. We now meet in the kitchen at the sports centre, where there is step-free access and a bus stop outside. The project is open to adults, but our largest group of volunteers is currently made up of teenagers, many of whom want experience before taking hospitality courses.||New volunteers sometimes worry that they must already know lots of dishes. In fact, we start with basic skills, such as cutting vegetables carefully and measuring ingredients. Nobody needs to remember instructions: each learner is given a set of recipe cards to take home and use again. Before touching any ingredients, all participants complete a brief form. We do not ask about favourite meals, but it is essential to tell us about food allergies, because we need to keep everyone safe. A trained leader supervises each group, and knives are only used after a demonstration.||Most of the food we prepare is for local people who cannot easily cook for themselves. In the first few sessions, we made pasta dishes, but these are not always easy to deliver without spilling. The meal requested most frequently by the organisation we support is vegetable soup, as it can be frozen and reheated easily. All ingredients are provided, and volunteers do not need to bring pans or other equipment. For hygiene reasons, everyone is issued with clean aprons at the beginning of a session; you may bring your own, but it must be washed before use.||Once the food is prepared, it must reach the right people. We originally expected volunteers to take food on foot to nearby houses, but some journeys are too far and the meals are heavy. Instead, the project works with a local delivery service that transports labelled containers each afternoon. Meals that are not needed immediately are put in a large freezer in our storeroom, where the date and ingredients are recorded. Learners are welcome to stay after cooking and help with labelling, though this is not compulsory. At the end of June, volunteers and the families we support will meet in the park for a picnic. Last year we had a formal evening meal, but people found the outdoor event more relaxed and easier for children to attend. There will be music, games and, naturally, food made by our teams. Anyone who joins before May can help plan it. I hope this has given you a clear idea of the programme, and I will now take any questions about joining.
